content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string AAA (containing lowercase Latin letters only) of length NNN where NNN is **even**. He can perform the following operation any number of times:


Swap AiA_iAi​ and Ai+2A_{i + 2}Ai+2​ for any 1≤i≤(N−2)1 \le i \le (N - 2)1≤i≤(N−2)
Determine if Chef can convert string AAA to a palindromic string.

**Note**: A string is called a palindrome if it reads the same backwards and forwards. For example, noon\texttt{noon}noon and level\texttt{level}level are palindromic strings but ebb\texttt{ebb}ebb is not.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the string AAA.
The second line of each test case contains a string AAA of length NNN containing lowercase Latin letters only.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if Chef can convert the string AAA to a palindromic string. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: S contains only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (even), the length of the string. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase Latin letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Chef can transform the string into a palindrome using the allowed swaps, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: odd positions a,b,a and even positions a,b,a match, so YES.\nTest case 2: odd positions a,c vs even b,d differ, so NO.\nTest case 3: odd positions z,x,y and even positions z,y,x have the same multiset, so YES.
First case: both positions contain 'a', counts equal → YES.\nSecond case: odd positions a,c, even positions b,a – character 'b' appears only on even side, mismatch → NO.
Odd indices: a,c,b,d (a,c,b,d) each appears once; even indices: b,d,a,c each appears once – all counts equal, so YES.

solution.approach: The operation swaps characters that are two positions apart, i.e., it only swaps characters within the same parity class (odd indices with odd indices, even with even). Therefore, any permutation of characters inside the odd positions and any permutation inside the even positions is achievable. In a palindrome of even length, each position i (1‑based) is paired with position N‑i+1, which always has opposite parity. Consequently, for each character its occurrences on odd positions must equal its occurrences on even positions. The algorithm counts, for every letter, how many times it appears on odd indices and on even indices. If all counts match, the answer is YES; otherwise NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a fixed 26×2 integer array is used.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        s = next(it)
        cnt = [[0,0] for _ in range(26)]
        for i,ch in enumerate(s):
            p = i % 2  # 0 -> odd position (1‑based)
            cnt[ord(ch)-97][p] += 1
        ok = all(cnt[c][0] == cnt[c][1] for c in range(26))
        out_lines.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
